spec.emotional_state: hopeful, made some progress on a goal
spec.label: A02_same_day

profile.personality: curious but reserved; thinks before replying; warms up after a few turns

transcript.content: Hi Priyansh, I heard you're planning to watch Dunki with English subtitles in two days — are you looking forward to it?
Yeah, I like Shah Rukh Khan movies. Maybe the subtitles will help me learn new words. But I am a bit nervous about understanding the fast parts. Have you seen it?
That’s a great plan — movies are a fun way to learn. Watching with subtitles is smart. What part of the movie are you most excited about?

The action scenes, I think. They usually have a lot of drama and emotions. I hope the subtitles make it easier to follow. Oh, and the comedy parts — I don’t want to miss the jokes! What about you, have you watched Dunki?
I get that — action and comedy are the best parts! I haven’t watched it yet, but I’ll ask: what made you pick it?

The trailer looked fun and it's by Rajkumar Hirani, so I thought why not. Plus, SRK's character seems interesting. I want to see how the story mixes comedy with something serious. Maybe the dialogue will help me learn how to express emotions better in English.
That’s a smart choice — Rajkumar Hirani’s stories always mix comedy and heart. What’s one emotion you’d like to learn to express better?

Hmm... maybe "excitement"? I want to learn how to say when I am really excited about something, like when a movie starts or when I achieve a goal. I tend to just say "I am happy" but I think there are better words.
That’s a great word to focus on — “excitement” is tricky but fun to learn. How do you usually express excitement in Hindi?

In Hindi, I often say "bhot khushi" or "dil kud kud ke roye" for big excitement. But I want to sound more natural in English. Like when I score a big win at work, I want to say more than just "I am happy." Maybe "I am thrilled" or "I am pumped"? I need to practice!
That sounds right — “thrilled” and “pumped” both work. “Thrilled” is a bit more formal, while “pumped” is casual and energetic. Which one fits your big win better?
I think "thrilled" sounds better for work achievements — maybe "pumped" for a cricket win or something fun! I'll try to use both in different situations. Thanks for explaining, Maya!
You're welcome — and great thinking! Let me know how it goes when you use "thrilled" next week. What else are you excited about this month?

I'm excited about a new project at work — it's a bit challenging but feels like a good growth opportunity. Maybe I'll say I'm "thrilled" when I talk about it in the next meeting! I should go now — thanks for the chat, Maya!
You're doing great! Use "thrilled" — it'll sound confident and natural. Tell me how the meeting goes later. Have a great day!
